content.text: The Vision Transformer (ViT) represents a paradigm shift in computer vision: rather than processing images through convolutional filters that exploit local spatial structure, it divides images into a sequence of fixed patches and feeds them through a standard Transformer encoder—the same architecture originally designed for natural language. This seemingly simple reframing unlocked a new class of scalable, transfer-learning-friendly visual models.

ViT applies the Transformer architecture, developed by Vaswani et al. (2017) for NLP, directly to 2D images with minimal modification. The key insight is that an image can be treated as a sequence: chop it into non-overlapping rectangular patches, flatten each patch into a vector, and process the resulting sequence with multi-head self-attention. The resulting model has no inductive bias for spatial locality—it learns global relationships between patches from the data itself.

The original ViT paper ("An Image is Worth 16x16 Words", Dosovitskiy et al., 2021) demonstrated that when trained at sufficient scale on large enough datasets, this approach matches or exceeds convolutional neural network (CNN) performance, while being more computationally efficient at high resolution and more naturally amenable to modern hardware due to its reliance on matrix multiplications.

CNNs dominated computer vision for nearly a decade due to their built-in inductive biases: local connectivity (convolutions only look at nearby pixels), weight sharing (the same filter is applied at every spatial location), and translation equivariance. These biases were essential when datasets were small, since they dramatically reduced the effective model complexity.
ViT challenges the assumption that these biases are necessary. By removing spatial priors and instead learning all relationships from data via attention, ViT:
within a single layer, something CNNs require many stacked layers to achieve
Scales more predictably with compute and data, following the familiar neural scaling laws from NLP
that transfer exceptionally well across diverse downstream tasks via fine-tuning
Integrates naturally with the Transformer ecosystem—the same infrastructure, optimizers, and pre-training techniques used for large language models (LLMs) can be reused
The deeper significance is that ViT began unifying vision and language architectures, opening the door to multimodal models like CLIP, Flamingo, GPT-4V, and Gemini.
The ViT architecture passes an image through several distinct stages before producing a classification output (or, in other uses, a rich feature representation).### Stage 1 — Patch Embedding

Given an image of size H×W×C (height, width, channels), ViT divides it into a grid of N non-overlapping patches of size P×P pixels. The number of patches is N = (H/P) × (W/P). The most common patch size used in the original paper is P=16, giving N=196 patches for a 224×224 image.

Each patch is flattened into a 1D vector of dimensionality P²·C (for P=16 and C=3, that's 768 values). This vector is then projected into the model's embedding dimension D via a learned linear transformation (a simple matrix multiplication):
x_i = flatten(patch_i) @ W_proj + b_proj   # shape: [N, D]

This linear projection layer is the ViT's only concession to learning a patch-level representation—there is no convolution involved. It is mathematically equivalent to a single convolutional layer with kernel size P×P, stride P, and D output channels applied to the image.
Stage 2 — Positional Encoding and the CLS Token

Because Transformers are permutation-invariant (attention treats the sequence as an unordered set), the model has no way to distinguish the spatial arrangement of patches without explicit positional information. ViT adds a learnable 1D positional embedding to each patch embedding:
z_i = x_i + pos_embed[i]   # pos_embed shape: [N+1, D]

The positional embeddings are 1D (indexed by patch position, 0 to N), not 2D. Despite this seeming limitation, ViT learns implicit 2D structure in these 1D embeddings through training—if you visualize the cosine similarity between positional embeddings, nearby patches cluster together.

is prepended to the patch sequence. Borrowed from BERT, this special token attends to all patches during self-attention. After all encoder blocks, only the CLS token's output is used for classification, since it aggregates information from the entire image.
sequence = [cls_token, patch_embed_1, patch_embed_2, ..., patch_embed_N]  # length N+1
The patch sequence is passed through L stacked Transformer encoder blocks. Each block contains:
Self-attention allows every token to attend to every other token. For each attention head h, the block computes queries Q, keys K, and values V from the input via linear projections, then computes:

where d_k is the per-head dimension (D divided by the number of heads). Multiple heads run in parallel; their outputs are concatenated and projected back to dimension D. In ViT-Base, there are 12 heads, each with d_k = 64.

The key property: every patch can attend to every other patch in O(N²) complexity with respect to sequence length. For N=196 patches (224×224, P=16), this is manageable, but it scales quadratically—a significant cost at higher resolutions.
After attention, a two-layer MLP with GELU activation processes each token independently. The intermediate dimension is typically 4×D:
FFN(x) = GELU(x @ W1 + b1) @ W2 + b2   # W1: [D, 4D], W2: [4D, D]
each sub-layer, not after), which stabilizes training at large scales:

x = x + MHSA(LayerNorm(x))
x = x + FFN(LayerNorm(x))
The residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the network, enabling training of deep stacks (L=12 to L=24 layers in standard configurations).
After L encoder blocks, the CLS token output
is passed through a simple MLP classification head:
During pre-training on large datasets (e.g., JFT-300M), the head is a two-layer MLP. During fine-tuning on smaller datasets (e.g., ImageNet), it is replaced with a single linear layer.
The original paper defines three main variants based on depth and embedding dimension:

The core finding of the ViT paper is that ViT requires significantly more data than CNNs to achieve competitive performance when trained from scratch. This is a direct consequence of the weaker inductive bias: without the locality prior, the model must learn spatial relationships purely from examples.

ViT was originally trained on JFT-300M (an internal Google dataset with 300 million images across ~18,000 categories). On ImageNet-21k (~14M images), smaller ViT variants can achieve strong performance. Training from scratch on the 1.28M images of ImageNet-1k yields mediocre results compared to ResNets of similar size—but fine-tuning a ViT pre-trained on JFT-300M or ImageNet-21k onto ImageNet-1k gives state-of-the-art results.

This data hunger can be offset by:
- Using

(Data-efficient Image Transformers), which adds knowledge distillation from a CNN teacher to enable training on ImageNet-1k alone
- Using
(MAE, DINO, DINOv2), which can extract useful representations from unlabeled images
A typical ViT pre-training setup (post-DeiT, post-MAE era) looks like:

# Pseudocode for ViT training setup
optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=0.1)
scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=num_epochs)

# Augmentations
transforms = Compose([
    RandomResizedCrop(224),
    RandomHorizontalFlip(),
    RandAugment(num_ops=2, magnitude=9),
    ColorJitter(brightness=0.4, contrast=0.4, saturation=0.4),
    Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
])

# Key training details
batch_size = 4096        # Large batch typical for ViT
warmup_epochs = 5        # LR warmup from 0
total_epochs = 300       # For ImageNet training
label_smoothing = 0.1    # Regularization
stochastic_depth = 0.1   # Drop path rate (increases with model size)

: ViT is sensitive to weight decay and learning rate. AdamW with weight decay 0.1 and a learning rate of around 1e-3 (scaled with batch size via linear or sqrt scaling) is standard. The weight decay should
apply to positional embeddings, bias terms, or LayerNorm parameters—these are excluded from the decay group.
When fine-tuning a pre-trained ViT on a downstream dataset, several adjustments are needed:

: ViT is often pre-trained at 224×224 but fine-tuned at higher resolutions (384×384) for better accuracy. The number of patches N increases with resolution. Since positional embeddings were trained for N=196 patches, they must be
to the new grid size using 2D bicubic interpolation—this is done in the patch embedding space.
: The pre-training MLP head is discarded and replaced with a new linear layer matching the number of downstream classes.

: Fine-tuning typically uses learning rates 10× to 100× smaller than pre-training (e.g., 1e-5 to 1e-4), often with layer-wise learning rate decay (deeper layers closer to the input get smaller learning rates).

The most impactful development after the original ViT paper is the discovery that masked image modeling enables highly effective self-supervised pre-training of ViTs, analogous to masked language modeling (BERT) for text.
MAE (He et al., 2022) masks a high fraction of image patches (typically 75%) and trains a ViT encoder-decoder pair to reconstruct the masked pixels from only the visible patches:

# MAE process
visible_patches = random_sample(all_patches, keep_ratio=0.25)
encoded = vit_encoder(visible_patches)        # Only processes 25% of patches
decoded = lightweight_decoder(encoded + mask_tokens)
loss = MSE(decoded[masked_positions], original_pixels[masked_positions])

The key insight is that images have far more redundancy than text, so a high masking ratio (75-80%) forces the model to learn genuine semantic representations rather than trivially interpolating neighboring pixels. The decoder is lightweight and discarded after pre-training—only the encoder is kept.

MAE achieves several things simultaneously:
- It requires no labels (fully self-supervised)
- The encoder never sees the mask tokens, so it processes only 25% of patches—making pre-training 3× faster than standard ViT training
- The resulting representations transfer very well to downstream tasks

DINO (Self-DIstillation with NO labels) uses a student-teacher framework where both the student and teacher are ViTs. The teacher is updated via an exponential moving average (EMA) of the student's weights. Given two augmented views of an image:

teacher_output = teacher(augment_global(image))  # teacher sees full global view
student_output = student(augment_local(image))   # student sees small crops
loss = CrossEntropy(teacher_output, student_output)  # match distributions

DINO produces features that spontaneously segment objects—the self-attention maps of a DINO-trained ViT highlight semantically meaningful object regions without any segmentation supervision. DINOv2 scales this up with a curated dataset (LVD-142M), producing features that transfer extremely well to depth estimation, segmentation, and dense prediction tasks.
Understanding what ViT's attention layers actually compute provides intuition for why the architecture works.

In early layers, attention heads tend to act locally—attending to nearby patches, which approximates what convolutions do. In deeper layers, attention becomes global, allowing the model to integrate information across distant image regions in a single computational step. This is fundamentally different from CNNs, where long-range dependencies can only be captured after many layers of convolution stack up a large effective receptive field.

The attention pattern for the [CLS] token is particularly diagnostic: it learns to attend to the most semantically informative patches in the image. In a DINO-trained ViT, this produces strikingly clean object segmentations when visualized.

# Extracting attention maps from a ViT (Hugging Face)
from transformers import ViTModel, ViTFeatureExtractor
import torch

model = ViTModel.from_pretrained('google/vit-base-patch16-224', output_attentions=True)
outputs = model(pixel_values=image_tensor)

# outputs.attentions: tuple of [B, num_heads, N+1, N+1] tensors, one per layer
last_layer_attn = outputs.attentions[-1]          # [B, 12, 197, 197]
cls_attn = last_layer_attn[0, :, 0, 1:]           # [12, 196] — CLS attending to patches
avg_cls_attn = cls_attn.mean(0).reshape(14, 14)   # Average over heads, reshape to grid
1. Training ViT from scratch on small datasets.

ViT-Base trained from scratch on ImageNet-1k significantly underperforms a comparably-sized ResNet. ViT requires either large datasets or strong pre-training. Always start from a pre-trained checkpoint.
2. Forgetting to interpolate positional embeddings when changing resolution.

If you load a ViT pre-trained at 224×224 and fine-tune at a different resolution without interpolating the positional embeddings, the model will receive malformed inputs for the positions outside the original range.
3. Applying weight decay to all parameters uniformly.
LayerNorm parameters, bias terms, and positional embeddings should typically be excluded from weight decay. Including them causes subtle performance degradation.

ViT training benefits from large batch sizes (4096+) because the gradient estimates are noisier without the spatial smoothing that convolutions implicitly provide. Linear learning rate scaling with batch size helps but may need warmup.

Smaller patches (ViT-B/8 vs ViT-B/16) dramatically increase N and thus quadratic attention cost, but can significantly improve accuracy on dense prediction tasks. Choose patch size deliberately based on the compute budget and task requirements.
Deeper ViT models (Large, Huge) benefit significantly from stochastic depth regularization. Without it, very deep ViTs tend to overfit or train poorly.

ViT follows power-law scaling: performance improves predictably as model size, dataset size, and compute budget increase. This property—shared with large language models—means that investing in pre-training on larger datasets or scaling up model size yields predictable returns. ViT-Huge trained on JFT-3B achieves over 90% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k.

The dominant cost in a ViT encoder is the self-attention computation, which scales as O(N²·D) per layer, where N is the number of patches. For a 224×224 image with P=16, N=196, which is manageable. But for high-resolution tasks (segmentation at 1024×1024 or detection with small objects), N can reach 4096 or more—making standard ViT prohibitively expensive.

: Attention is computed only within local windows (e.g., 7×7 patches), restoring O(N) complexity. Cross-window communication is achieved through shifted windows in alternating layers. Swin becomes the de facto backbone for dense prediction tasks.

: Linformer, Performer, and similar methods approximate the attention matrix, reducing complexity to O(N·D). However, they often sacrifice some accuracy and are less commonly used in practice than window attention.

: Models like Swin, PVT (Pyramid Vision Transformer), and MViT build multi-scale feature hierarchies analogous to CNNs—early stages process fine-grained patches at high resolution, later stages operate on coarser representations—enabling efficient use across detection and segmentation heads.
: The primary Python library for using pre-trained ViTs. Covers ViT, DeiT, Swin, BEiT, DINOv2, and dozens of variants:

from transformers import ViTForImageClassification, ViTFeatureExtractor
model = ViTForImageClassification.from_pretrained('google/vit-base-patch16-224')
: Ross Wightman's library contains hundreds of ViT variants with pre-trained weights, often outperforming the original implementations:

import timm
model = timm.create_model('vit_base_patch16_224', pretrained=True)
model_ft = timm.create_model('vit_large_patch16_384', pretrained=True, num_classes=10)
: The original ViT codebase from Google Brain is JAX-based. For TPU training, JAX's
, and XLA compilation give significant throughput advantages over PyTorch.
: OpenMMLab's libraries integrate ViT backbones into structured training pipelines for classification, detection, and segmentation with clean configuration files.

CNNs like ResNet and EfficientNet remain competitive at small-to-medium scale and are faster at inference. ViTs pull ahead on large-scale pre-training and tasks requiring global context. Swin occupies the middle ground, offering ViT-level pre-training compatibility with CNN-like efficiency for dense prediction. ConvNeXt (a CNN redesigned by borrowing ideas from ViT training recipes) is a strong alternative when deployment efficiency is critical and the ViT attention mechanism is not needed.
Match patch size to task requirements

: Use P=16 for classification (good speed/accuracy tradeoff), P=8 or P=4 for dense prediction tasks (more patches, higher resolution feature maps), and hierarchical variants (Swin) when you need feature pyramids.
: Even if your target domain differs from ImageNet, pre-trained ViTs transfer well. LAION-2B or JFT pre-trained checkpoints outperform ImageNet-only pre-training as starting points.
Use MAE or DINO pre-training for domain-specific unlabeled data

: If fine-grained domain data (satellite imagery, medical scans, microscopy) is available without labels, a round of self-supervised pre-training before supervised fine-tuning often significantly outperforms direct fine-tuning from ImageNet weights.
Tune weight decay and learning rate carefully
: ViT is more sensitive to these than CNNs. A sweep over LR in {5e-5, 1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3} and weight decay in {0.01, 0.05, 0.1} is advisable when establishing a new fine-tuning setup.
: ViTs train well in float16 (with loss scaling) or bfloat16 (on TPUs/newer GPUs with native BF16 support). This roughly halves memory use and increases throughput with minimal accuracy impact.
Profile before switching to window attention
: For many classification tasks, standard ViT at P=16 is fast enough. Only switch to Swin or similar when N is large or fine-grained spatial features are needed.
Self-attention allows every patch to attend to every other patch in a single encoder block. CNNs require many stacked layers to achieve the same effective receptive field.
ViT follows the same power-law scaling as LLMs: more compute and data consistently improves performance. ViT-Huge trained on JFT-3B exceeds 90% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet.

ViT uses identical Transformer blocks to language models. The same infrastructure, optimisers, and pre-training techniques apply — enabling multimodal models that share weights between vision and text processing.
MAE and DINOv2 pre-training produces features that transfer to downstream tasks with very little labelled data — often outperforming supervised pre-training when fine-tuning data is scarce.
High-resolution fine-tuning via positional embedding interpolation.
ViT pre-trained at 224×224 can be fine-tuned at 384×384 or 512×512 by interpolating positional embeddings, gaining accuracy at the cost of compute.

ViT trained from scratch on ImageNet-1k (1.28M images) significantly underperforms ResNets of similar size. The weak spatial inductive bias means more data or self-supervised pre-training is required to learn visual representations.
Standard ViT attention scales as O(N²·D) per layer. For N=196 (224×224, P=16) this is manageable, but N=4096 (1024×1024, P=16) is prohibitively expensive.

CNN feature pyramids (multi-scale features) are well-suited to dense prediction tasks like detection and segmentation. Standard ViT produces single-scale features — adapting it to dense prediction requires additional components (e.g., global average pooling or a multi-scale decoder).

ViT is more sensitive to learning rate, weight decay, and augmentation than CNNs. Without the spatial inductive bias to regularise representations, incorrect hyperparameters lead to significant performance degradation.

ViT training benefits from batch sizes of 4096–16384 for stable gradient estimates. This requires distributed training across multiple GPUs, raising the compute requirement compared to CNN training at equivalent resolution.
Large-scale pretraining data is available (JFT, LAION, ImageNet-21k) or self-supervised pre-training (MAE, DINO) is planned — ViT's benefits over CNNs are most pronounced at scale.
You need multimodal compatibility — ViT's identical Transformer blocks to LLMs enable direct integration in CLIP, Flamingo, GPT-4V-style architectures.
Global context in each layer is important — tasks where long-range dependencies matter from early processing (e.g., medical imaging where distant regions correlate).
You are starting from a high-quality pre-trained checkpoint (timm, Hugging Face) and fine-tuning — ViTs transfer excellently, making the compute investment in pre-training amortisable.
Training from scratch on medium-sized datasets (<5M images) — CNNs learn useful features with less data due to spatial inductive biases.
Dense prediction tasks (detection, segmentation) with very high-resolution inputs — use Swin or similar hierarchical variants that provide multi-scale features with linear attention complexity.
Edge/mobile deployment — CNNs and MobileNets are better optimised for inference on CPU and mobile hardware; most ViT optimised runtimes target GPU/TPU.
Real-World Example: Fine-tuning DINOv2 for Medical Imaging

DINOv2 ViT-L/14 — trained self-supervised on LVD-142M without labels — provides features that transfer strongly even to out-of-domain images like medical scans. The following shows end-to-end fine-tuning for histopathology slide classification:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.optim import AdamW
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import CosineAnnealingLR
from torchvision import transforms
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from datasets import load_dataset

# 1. Load DINOv2 ViT-L/14 from timm
import timm

class DINOv2Classifier(nn.Module):
    """Fine-tune DINOv2 ViT-L/14 for a classification task."""
    def __init__(self, num_classes: int, freeze_backbone: bool = False):
        super().__init__()
        # DINOv2 ViT-L/14 — trained self-supervised on LVD-142M
        self.backbone = torch.hub.load("facebookresearch/dinov2", "dinov2_vitl14")
        embed_dim = self.backbone.embed_dim  # 1024 for ViT-L

        if freeze_backbone:
            for param in self.backbone.parameters():
                param.requires_grad = False

        # Lightweight classification head
        self.head = nn.Sequential(
            nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim),
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, num_classes),
        )

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # DINOv2 returns CLS token when forward_features is called
        features = self.backbone(x)  # (B, embed_dim) — CLS token output
        return self.head(features)


# 2. Training setup — key ViT-specific settings
model = DINOv2Classifier(num_classes=2)  # e.g., tumour / non-tumour

# Layer-wise learning rate decay: deeper (closer to input) gets smaller LR
def get_param_groups(model, base_lr=1e-4, decay=0.75):
    backbone_layers = list(model.backbone.blocks)  # List of encoder blocks
    total_layers = len(backbone_layers)

    param_groups = []
    # Head gets full LR
    param_groups.append({"params": model.head.parameters(), "lr": base_lr})

    # Each encoder layer gets decayed LR: deeper = smaller LR
    for layer_idx, layer in enumerate(reversed(backbone_layers)):
        layer_lr = base_lr * (decay ** (layer_idx + 1))
        param_groups.append({"params": layer.parameters(), "lr": layer_lr})

    return param_groups

optimizer = AdamW(
    get_param_groups(model),
    weight_decay=0.05,
)

# 3. Preprocessing for ViT/14 (224×224 patches of 14 pixels)
preprocess = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.Resize(518),           # DINOv2 ViT-L/14 prefers 518 (37×37 patches)
    transforms.CenterCrop(518),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
])

# 4. Training loop
scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=20, eta_min=1e-6)

def train_epoch(model, loader, optimizer, device):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0.0
    for imgs, labels in loader:
        imgs, labels = imgs.to(device), labels.to(device)
        logits = model(imgs)
        loss = nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits, labels, label_smoothing=0.1)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
    return total_loss / len(loader)

# 5. Positional embedding interpolation for different resolutions
# If you want to fine-tune at 518 but the backbone was pre-trained at 224:
def interpolate_pos_embed(model, new_size: int):
    """Interpolate ViT positional embeddings to a new image size."""
    # DINOv2 ViT-L/14: patch_size=14, so 518/14 = 37 patches per side
    patch_size = model.backbone.patch_size  # 14
    old_n = model.backbone.pos_embed.shape[1] - 1  # subtract CLS token
    old_size = int(old_n ** 0.5)                    # e.g., 16 for 224/14=16
    new_n = (new_size // patch_size) ** 2           # e.g., 37*37=1369 for 518/14

    if old_n == new_n:
        return  # No interpolation needed

    pos_embed = model.backbone.pos_embed
    cls_token = pos_embed[:, :1, :]         # (1, 1, D) — CLS stays unchanged
    patch_embed = pos_embed[:, 1:, :]       # (1, old_n, D)

    # Reshape to 2D spatial grid and interpolate
    D = patch_embed.shape[-1]
    patch_embed = patch_embed.reshape(1, old_size, old_size, D).permute(0, 3, 1, 2)
    patch_embed = torch.nn.functional.interpolate(
        patch_embed, size=(new_size // patch_size, new_size // patch_size),
        mode="bicubic", align_corners=False,
    )
    patch_embed = patch_embed.permute(0, 2, 3, 1).reshape(1, new_n, D)

    model.backbone.pos_embed = nn.Parameter(torch.cat([cls_token, patch_embed], dim=1))
    print(f"Interpolated positional embeddings: {old_size}x{old_size} → {new_size//patch_size}x{new_size//patch_size}")

ViTs are not immune to adversarial examples. While they are somewhat more robust than CNNs to high-frequency pixel perturbations (due to patch-level processing), they are vulnerable to patch-level adversarial attacks. For security-critical vision systems, apply certified defences (randomised smoothing) regardless of backbone.
Positional embedding interpolation can silently degrade accuracy.

If you change resolution at inference without interpolating positional embeddings (or forget to add the interpolation step in a saved model's forward pass), accuracy degrades silently — no error is thrown. Always test at multiple resolutions after making resolution changes.
CLS token can leak global context too aggressively.

In multi-image batch processing, ensure the CLS token does not cross-contaminate between samples. The causal mask in NLP prevents this naturally; in ViT, batch independence is guaranteed by PyTorch's batched matrix multiplication, but custom attention implementations must be audited.

ViT weights saved with torch.save() use pickle by default. Use safetensors (from huggingface_hub or timm) for security-sensitive deployments, as pickle files loaded from untrusted sources can execute arbitrary code.
Ensure dropout is set to eval mode (model.eval()) at inference. Leaving dropout active at inference causes stochastic predictions — a reliability issue in production that is easy to miss.
ViT divides images into fixed-size patches, treats each patch as a token, and applies a standard Transformer encoder to the resulting sequence—replacing convolutional feature extraction entirely.
The [CLS] token prepended to the sequence aggregates global image information across all encoder layers and is used as the sole input to the classification head.

ViT's weak spatial inductive bias means it requires significantly more training data than CNNs to learn competitive representations—either large labeled datasets (JFT-300M, ImageNet-21k) or self-supervised pre-training (MAE, DINO).

Self-supervised methods, especially MAE (masked autoencoding at 75% masking) and DINO (self-distillation), have largely solved the data requirement problem and produce transferable representations that rival or exceed supervised pre-training.
Attention complexity scales as O(N²) in the sequence length, making standard ViT expensive at high resolution; hierarchical variants like Swin address this for dense prediction tasks.

ViT's architectural alignment with LLMs (identical Transformer blocks) makes it a natural building block for multimodal models—the same encoder can be combined with language decoders to enable visual question answering, image captioning, and instruction following.
and Hugging Face provide strong starting points; fine-tuning with lower learning rates and layer-wise LR decay is the standard adaptation strategy for downstream tasks.
